 ## 1. Foundation Layer
 
+### Component Framework
 Keep shadcn/ui as the structural layer. Use React Bits only for targeted motion (page transitions, sheet entrances). Do not adopt Tailark. Ground every decision in existing components: SearchPage, DocumentPage, BottomSheet, CommandPalette, DocumentGraph, MobileActionsBar.
 
+### State Management
+URL is the source of truth for search state (query, filters, pagination). Zustand for client-side ephemeral state (sheet open/closed, reading mode toggle, active section). localStorage for persistence (reading positions, saved searches, typography preferences). No React Context for anything that crosses more than two component levels — Zustand stores are cheaper and debuggable.
+
+**Deliverable:** `src/stores/` directory with `searchStore.ts`, `readingStore.ts`, `preferencesStore.ts`. URL sync via a custom `useURLSearchParams` hook that bidirectionally syncs Zustand search state with `window.location.search`.
+

+### Persistence Rules
+- Cap at 100 reading positions (LRU eviction) and 50 saved searches.
+- All localStorage operations wrapped in try/catch. On `QuotaExceededError`: evict oldest 20% of reading positions, retry. If still fails, degrade to sessionStorage for current session with a non-modal toast: "Storage full. Reading position will not be saved."
+- Schema migration utility runs on app init: reads `_schemaVersion`, applies migrations sequentially. Unknown versions are discarded with a console warning.
+- Staleness detection: if `documentHash` doesn't match current document metadata, discard the reading position and don't show the resume prompt.
+
+### Post-MVP Migration Path
+When backend user service ships: a `migrateLocalStorageToBackend()` function runs on first authenticated session, uploads all reading positions and saved searches, then clears localStorage copies. Design the unauthenticated experience so it feels complete, not degraded.

-DOU documents can exceed 50,000 words.

-- **Threshold:** Documents under 5,000 words render fully. Documents over 5,000 words use chunked rendering.
-- **Strategy:** Render first 3 sections immediately. Remaining sections render as the user scrolls within 2 sections of them (IntersectionObserver with rootMargin). No react-window — legal documents need contiguous DOM for Ctrl+F browser search to work. Chunked append is the right tradeoff.

+DOU documents can exceed 50,000 words. The rendering strategy is tiered based on the §0 document size distribution data.
+
+**Tier 1 — Documents under 5,000 words (~P50):**
+Full render. No chunking. Browser Ctrl+F works completely.
+
+**Tier 2 — Documents 5,000–20,000 words (~P50–P90):**
+Chunked append via IntersectionObserver. Render first 5 sections immediately; append remaining sections as user scrolls within 2 sections of them. Accept DOM node counts up to 8,000. Browser Ctrl+F works for rendered content; unrendered sections hydrate quickly enough on forward scroll that most Ctrl+F "find next" operations work. Log node counts to monitoring but do not alert.
+
+**Tier 3 — Documents over 20,000 words (~P90+):**
+Windowed rendering: render visible sections ±2, unmount sections more than 4 screens away. Browser Ctrl+F is explicitly degraded for these documents. Replace with an **in-app document search bar** (`src/components/DocumentSearch.tsx`) that searches the raw text/JSON model (kept in memory, never in DOM) and scrolls to matches, hydrating target sections on demand. The in-app search bar is shown automatically for Tier 3 documents and available via Cmd+F override (intercept the keyboard shortcut) or a search icon in the sticky header.
+
+- Show a subtle banner on Tier 3 documents: "Documento extenso — use a busca interna (Ctrl+F) para localizar termos."
+- **Memory management:** Unmounted sections release their DOM nodes. The raw text model (~1 byte per character) for a 50,000-word document is ~300KB — acceptable to keep in memory.
+- **CSS containment:** All section wrappers use `contain: content` to isolate layout/paint costs.

objections.description: The plan rejects react-window to preserve Ctrl+F but then proposes chunked DOM append via IntersectionObserver. For documents exceeding 50,000 words this still means the full DOM eventually materializes — the 5,000 DOM node budget in §9 is mathematically incompatible with rendering a 50k-word legal document that has nested structure, mark highlights, and anchor IDs. A single 50k-word document with article/paragraph/inciso nesting easily produces 8,000–15,000 nodes. There is no stated fallback or degradation strategy when the budget is exceeded, and the dev-mode alert is not a solution — it's a notification of failure. This is the core reading experience; shipping without a concrete reconciliation between 'contiguous DOM for Ctrl+F' and 'DOM budget of 5,000' will result in either broken search or broken performance on mid-range Android.
objections.suggested_fix: Define explicit tiers: (1) <5k words: full render. (2) 5k–20k words: chunked append, accept DOM budget overshoot up to 8k nodes, log but don't alert. (3) >20k words: render visible sections ±2, but ALSO implement a custom in-document search bar (Cmd+F equivalent) that searches the raw text/JSON model rather than relying on browser Ctrl+F. This lets you unmount off-screen sections without losing searchability. State clearly that browser Ctrl+F is degraded for tier 3 documents and the in-app search replaces it. Add the in-app document search component to the file list and timebox.

objections.location: Section 5a: Legal Hierarchy Rendering — flat HTML fallback
objections.description: The plan says 'Parse server-side or client-side using regex/DOM parsing for Art., §, Roman numeral incisos, letter alíneas' and then 'Degrade gracefully: if parsing fails, render as plain formatted text.' This is dangerously hand-wavy for the feature that defines the entire product. DOU documents have inconsistent formatting across decades of publications: some use 'Art.' some use 'ART.', paragraph markers vary (§, Parágrafo único), roman numerals can be uppercase or lowercase, and editorial notes/retificações break expected patterns. A regex-based parser with no test corpus and no stated coverage target will silently mis-parse documents, producing incorrect legal hierarchy — which for a government document product is worse than showing plain text. There is no mention of a parsing test suite or accuracy measurement.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a concrete deliverable: before building the parser, pull 20 diverse real documents via gabi-es (varying organs, decades, sections) and create a `tests/fixtures/dou-structure/` directory. Write the parser with these as test cases. Define an acceptance threshold: parser must correctly identify article boundaries in ≥90% of test fixtures. Documents that fail parsing get plain-text rendering with a visible 'structured view unavailable' indicator, not silent degradation. Add 2 days to the viewer timebox for parser development and test fixture creation.

objections.description: The plan proposes runtime regex parsing of cross-references like 'Art. 5° da Lei 8.666/93' and then a live lookup via gabi-es to check corpus existence. This is an N+1 query pattern executed during document render. A 50-article regulatory document can easily contain 30–100 cross-references. Firing 30–100 Elasticsearch lookups on document open will blow the 2.0s FCP budget from §9 and create unpredictable load on the backend. There is no caching strategy, no batching strategy, and no stated limit on concurrent lookups.
objections.suggested_fix: Cross-reference resolution must be either: (A) precomputed at index time and stored as a field on the document (preferred — add to 'Requires backend work' column in §0), or (B) done as a single batch query after render (extract all reference patterns, fire one multi-search request, then decorate links asynchronously). Option B is acceptable for MVP but must not block initial render. Render all references as styled non-interactive text first, then upgrade to links as batch results return. Add this to the architecture and adjust the timebox.

objections.location: Section 4: Bottom Sheet Primitives — gesture conflicts
objections.description: The plan defines 'Drag on handle zone (top 48px) controls sheet position. Below handle, internal content scrolls normally' but does not address the well-known iOS Safari and Chrome mobile issue where overscroll-behavior on nested scrollable containers inside a sheet causes the sheet to move when the inner content reaches scroll boundaries. This is the single most common source of jank in bottom sheet implementations on mobile web and the 3-day timebox doesn't account for the browser-specific debugging this requires.
objections.suggested_fix: Explicitly state the overscroll containment strategy: use `overscroll-behavior: contain` on inner scroll containers, use touch-action manipulation on the handle zone, and test specifically on iOS Safari 16+ and Chrome Android 120+ where behavior differs. If the custom implementation proves fragile within 2 days, have a named fallback library (e.g., vaul by emilkowalski) that handles these edge cases. Add browser-specific testing to the done-when criteria.

objections.description: The plan specifies @tanstack/react-virtual for result sets over 50 items but doesn't address that virtualized lists break browser Ctrl+F (the same issue acknowledged for the document viewer). For search results this is a real usability problem — users searching legal documents frequently use Ctrl+F to scan result snippets for specific terms. Additionally, the plan doesn't specify what happens with URL-synced pagination: are results paginated server-side or is the full result set fetched and virtualized client-side? This has massive implications for backend load and time-to-first-result.
objections.suggested_fix: Clarify the pagination model: server-side pagination with page/cursor in URL params (preferred for a search product). If server-side paginated with 20–50 results per page, virtualization is unnecessary — just render the page. Reserve @tanstack/react-virtual only if you confirm the backend returns unbounded result sets with no pagination support. Add pagination model to the §0 backend audit questions.

objections.description: The reading position schema stores `scrollPercent` but the viewer uses section-based anchors for TOC and deep linking. Scroll percentage is fragile: it changes meaning if the document's rendered height changes (font size change, collapsed sections expanding, viewport resize). The resume prompt says 'Resume from Art. 15?' which implies section-based positioning, but the schema only has `lastSectionId` as nullable — suggesting scroll percent is the primary mechanism.
objections.suggested_fix: Make `lastSectionId` the primary resume mechanism, non-nullable for structured documents. Use `scrollPercent` only as a fallback for documents where section parsing failed. Update the schema to: `{ documentId: string, lastSectionId: string, scrollOffsetWithinSection: number, scrollPercentFallback: number, timestamp: number }`. This survives font size changes and collapsed section state changes.

objections.description: LCP < 1.5s on 4G throttle is aggressive for a search-and-read app that must fetch results from Elasticsearch. The plan doesn't distinguish between LCP on the home/empty state (achievable — it's mostly static) and LCP on a search results page or document viewer (which depends entirely on backend response time). A single Elasticsearch query on a large corpus can take 200–800ms, and 4G throttle adds ~150ms RTT. Combined with JS parse/eval on mid-range Android, 1.5s is likely not achievable for the search results route without server-side rendering or aggressive prefetching, neither of which is mentioned.
objections.suggested_fix: Split the LCP target by route: Home/empty state: <1.5s. Search results: <2.5s (with skeleton UI shown within 500ms). Document viewer: <2.0s for first section content (the rest loads progressively). Add skeleton/loading state components to the deliverable list — they are not mentioned anywhere in the plan but are critical for perceived performance.

objections.description: The plan removes the analytics tab and settings tab, leaving only Search and Saved. But the plan also introduces typography controls, reading mode toggle, and font controls — all of which are 'settings-like' features scattered across contextual locations. There's no discoverability story for these. A new user opening a long document has no way to know font size controls exist unless they tap through MobileActionsBar actions. For a professional reading tool, discoverability of reading comfort features is table stakes.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a brief onboarding tooltip or first-visit indicator on the MobileActionsBar's typography action (e.g., a subtle dot badge that clears on first use). This costs almost nothing to implement and solves the discoverability gap without adding a settings tab.

suggestions.location: Section 5: Document Viewer — missing offline/error states
suggestions.description: The viewer section has no mention of error handling: what happens when the document fetch fails (network error, 404, 500)? What about partial document loads (chunked rendering where one chunk fails to parse)? For a professional reading tool, these states need explicit design. Add: (1) Document fetch error → full-page error state with retry button, (2) Parse error for specific section → inline error placeholder within the document body ('Section could not be rendered'), (3) Network loss during reading → continue showing already-rendered content with an offline banner.

The empty state shows 'today's DOU publication count' and '5 most recent publications from each section.' This requires a dedicated backend endpoint or a date-filtered aggregation query that fires on every app open. If the user opens the app on a weekend or holiday when there are no publications, the empty state shows '0 new publications today' — which is a dead end. Design for the zero-publications case: fall back to 'most recent publications' without a date qualifier, and show the actual date of the latest publication.

The plan says 'dark-first' but never addresses light mode at all. Even if dark is the default, users reading dense legal text in daylight conditions will need a light mode. The design token architecture should include both themes from the start — retrofitting a light theme onto warm-gray dark tokens is painful because the semantic mapping (surface, elevation, emphasis levels) reverses. At minimum, define the light mode neutral scale alongside the dark scale in design-tokens.ts even if only the dark theme ships in MVP.

suggestions.location: Section 5g: Reading Mode — keyboard shortcut Alt+R
suggestions.description: Alt+R conflicts with browser-level shortcuts on some platforms (Alt activates the menu bar on Windows Firefox). Use a less collision-prone shortcut or make it configurable. Also, the plan mentions no keyboard shortcut reference/cheat sheet — for a tool with Cmd+K, Alt+R, Escape behaviors, and arrow key navigation, a discoverable shortcut reference (shown inside CommandPalette as a tab) would serve the power-user persona well.

The plan has no risk register or contingency planning. The biggest risk is §0 (backend audit) revealing that document bodies are unstructured raw text with no field-level filtering, which would invalidate §5a structured rendering, §5b TOC generation, §5d cross-references, and §6a filter UI. The plan should state: 'If §0 reveals flat text bodies with no field filtering, the viewer timebox increases by 3 days for parser development and the search timebox decreases by 2 days (fewer filter features). Total timeline impact: +1 day net.'

suggestions.location: Section 5: Document Viewer — text selection and copy
suggestions.description: For a legal document reader, text selection and copy-with-citation is a primary workflow. Users need to select a passage, copy it, and get the text plus a citation reference (e.g., 'Art. 5, § 3º — DOU Seção 1, 15/03/2024, p. 42'). The plan has no mention of text selection behavior, copy formatting, or citation generation. This is a differentiation opportunity that directly serves the professional user persona.

objections.description: Chunked append rendering while maintaining Ctrl+F browser search is technically contradictory. Browser find only works on rendered DOM nodes. If you render 3 sections initially for a 50,000-word document, Ctrl+F will not find matches in unrendered sections. This is a fundamental UX break for legal research workflows where users need to search within documents.
objections.suggested_fix: Either commit to full rendering with virtualization that supports search (react-virtual with getItemKey), or implement a custom search index that searches the full document text and scrolls to matches (loading chunks as needed). Document this tradeoff explicitly in backend-capabilities.md.

objections.description: 5,000 DOM node budget for 50,000-word documents is mathematically impossible without losing semantic structure. A single article with nested parágrafos/incisos/alíneas can easily exceed 50 nodes. At 5,000 nodes, you can render approximately 100 articles maximum. This constraint will force you to either break legal hierarchy rendering or exceed the budget.
objections.suggested_fix: Recalculate based on actual DOU document samples from gabi-es audit. Set node budget per-section rather than per-document, or increase budget to 15,000 nodes with aggressive CSS containment and will-change optimizations.

objections.description: Warm gray at hsl(30, 4%, N%) with N=4% for background-deepest will appear indistinguishable from pure black on most OLED mobile displays. The claimed 13:1 contrast ratio against 85% foreground needs verification - standard WCAG calculators show hsl(30, 4%, 4%) vs hsl(30, 4%, 85%) yields approximately 12.5:1, but this varies by display calibration. More critically, mid-range combinations flagged as potentially failing have no remediation plan.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a token validation script that runs in CI and fails the build if any text pair falls below 4.5:1. Specify fallback color pairs for each token that are guaranteed to pass. Test on actual iOS/Android devices, not just Chrome DevTools.

objections.description: Real-time gabi-es lookup for every cross-reference pattern on document render will destroy performance. A single DOU document can contain 200+ cross-references. Making 200 API calls per document load is not viable. The plan mentions 'precomputed link table' as an option but doesn't commit to it or specify who builds it.
objections.suggested_fix: Make precomputed cross-reference resolution a backend prerequisite (add to Section 0 audit). If backend cannot provide this, disable interactive cross-references in MVP and render as styled text only. Do not attempt client-side resolution.

objections.description: No migration strategy when auth is added post-MVP. Users will lose all reading positions and saved searches when moving from localStorage to backend sync. This creates a degraded experience for early adopters, contradicting the goal to 'design the unauthenticated experience so it feels complete, not degraded.'
objections.suggested_fix: Add a localStorage-to-backend migration hook that runs on first authenticated session. Preserve all existing reading positions and saved searches by uploading them during the auth handshake. Document this in the auth service spec.

objections.description: 3 days for three sheet variants with gesture conflict resolution is aggressive. iOS Safari's dynamic address bar and viewport height inconsistencies (100vh vs 100dvh) will break sheet positioning. No mention of testing on iOS 15-17 or Android Chrome's address bar behavior.
objections.suggested_fix: Add 2 days to timebox for cross-browser sheet testing. Use CSS custom property --webkit-fill-available or JavaScript viewport height detection. Add explicit iOS Safari testing to the 'Done when' criteria.

objections.description: LCP < 1.5s on mobile 4G with Source Serif 4 variable font is unrealistic. Variable fonts are 50-100kb minimum. With font-display: swap, you get FOUT on document navigation. With font-display: block, you block rendering. The initial bundle < 200kb gzip is also tight for React + shadcn/ui + multiple pages.
objections.suggested_fix: Relax LCP target to < 2.5s for document routes (where font loads). Preload Source Serif 4 on search page with low priority. Use font subsetting to reduce variable font size. Add font loading state UI to prevent layout shift.

objections.description: Zero-result recovery logic requires backend aggregation counts that may not exist. The plan says 'design now, ship when ready' but doesn't specify what the UI shows in the interim. Empty zero-result states will frustrate users during the gap between design and backend readiness.
objections.suggested_fix: Define a fallback zero-result state for Tier 1 that works without aggregations: show query spelling suggestions (client-side), show broadest filter option ('Search all sections'), and show contact/support link. Upgrade to Tier 2 UI when backend supports it.

objections.description: Automated contrast checks do not catch semantic accessibility issues. No mention of testing with actual screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver, TalkBack). Legal document structure (artigos, parágrafos, incisos) needs proper ARIA landmarks that automated tools won't validate.
objections.suggested_fix: Add manual screen reader testing to 'Done when' criteria for Document Viewer. Include at least one test session with VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android). Document expected screen reader output for article navigation.

objections.description: 18 days with 2 developers assumes no backend delays, no QA time, and no integration issues. Step 0 (backend audit) is marked as 1 day but depends on gabi-es availability and backend team responsiveness. No buffer for tool conflicts or MCP tool failures.
objections.suggested_fix: Add 5-day buffer to timeline. Explicitly define escalation path if gabi-es cannot answer audit questions within the timebox. Add QA pass (2 days) after Step 7 before considering the plan complete.

suggestions.description: Add specific questions about rate limiting, API response times for large documents, and whether the backend supports streaming responses for progressive document loading.

Add error state handling: what happens when a document fails to load, when cross-reference lookups timeout, or when the document structure is corrupted? Define retry logic and user-facing error messages.
Add localStorage quota monitoring. When approaching the 5MB limit, warn users and offer to export their reading history. Some users may have hundreds of saved searches.

Consider adding a minimal settings context for global preferences (default search section, default font size) even without a dedicated settings page. Store in localStorage with the same schema as viewer preferences.
Add bundle size monitoring per-route, not just initial route. Document route with Source Serif 4 should have its own budget. Add CI check that fails if any route exceeds 300kb gzip.

objections.description: The plan defers critical architectural decisions to the backend audit but then proceeds to design two parallel implementation paths. This is not 'grounded in the current codebase' — it's speculative. The entire document viewer architecture (TOC generation, section anchoring, collapse strategy) depends on whether the backend returns structured JSON or flat HTML. Designing both paths before the audit completes wastes effort and creates false confidence.
objections.suggested_fix: Move Section 5a implementation design AFTER Section 0 audit is complete. Replace the conditional with a single implementation approach that will be validated during the audit. If audit reveals flat HTML, add a dedicated parsing service with proper AST generation, not client-side regex.

objections.description: Chunked append strategy is underspecified for production. No specification for: (1) how IntersectionObserver interacts with browser Ctrl+F when not-yet-rendered content exists, (2) memory management when user scrolls through a 50,000-word document and DOM nodes accumulate, (3) how chunked rendering affects TOC scroll-sync and search highlight positioning. The claim 'no react-window because Ctrl+F' ignores that chunked append has the same problem — Ctrl+F won't find text in chunks not yet rendered.
objections.suggested_fix: Specify a hybrid approach: render all text content in a compressed form (hidden spans with textContent) for Ctrl+F compatibility, but only hydrate visible sections as rich DOM. Alternatively, pre-render entire document to an iframe with virtual scrolling. Address memory management explicitly — specify a maximum live DOM node budget and eviction strategy for scrolled-past content.

objections.description: localStorage persistence has no error handling, migration strategy, or corruption recovery. Reading position schema includes 'lastSectionId' but no document version hash — if a DOU document is republished with structural changes, stale section IDs will break navigation. The 100-item LRU cap doesn't account for localStorage quota errors or JSON serialization failures.
objections.suggested_fix: Add schema version field. Add document hash (publication date + ID) to reading positions for staleness detection. Wrap all localStorage operations in try/catch with quota-exceeded fallback. Add a schema migration utility that runs on app init. Specify what happens when localStorage is full (graceful degradation to session-only state).

objections.description: 18-24 working days is unrealistic for the scope. Document viewer alone (8 days) includes: progressive rendering system, TOC generation from parsed structure, search highlighting with navigation, cross-reference parsing and linking, URL deep linking, typography controls, reading progress persistence. Each of these is a non-trivial feature requiring testing. No time allocated for: code review, QA testing, accessibility testing with actual screen readers, performance regression testing, bug fixes from integration.
objections.suggested_fix: Add explicit time for testing and iteration: add 2 days after each major feature (viewer, search) for integration testing and bug fixes. Add 2 days total for accessibility testing with VoiceOver/TalkBack. Total realistic timeline: 26-32 days solo. Alternatively, cut scope by deferring cross-reference linking and reading progress persistence to a second phase.

objections.description: Regex parsing for legal references is notoriously unreliable. Brazilian legal citations include edge cases like 'Art. 5º-A', '§ 3º-B', 'Lei nº 8.666/93' vs 'Lei 8.666/1993' vs 'Lei nº 8.666, de 21 de junho de 1993'. No consideration for false positives (e.g., 'Art. 5' appearing in quoted text, not as a reference). Real-time lookup for each parsed reference could be slow.
objections.suggested_fix: Use a proper parser with grammar-based extraction (nearley.js or similar). Build a reference index at document ingestion time (backend) rather than parsing on every view. Cache resolved links per-document with TTL. For MVP, only link references within the same document; inter-document links are Tier 2.

objections.description: Client-side term matching fallback is underspecified. For a 50,000-word document, matching a query string against all text nodes is O(n) with significant memory pressure. No handling for: (1) stemming (licitação vs licitações), (2) case sensitivity, (3) phrase matching, (4) boolean operators in query. The 'N matches found' indicator could show misleading counts if client-side matching differs from backend highlight logic.
objections.suggested_fix: If backend doesn't return highlights, DO NOT attempt client-side matching for complex queries. Show a toast: 'Highlights unavailable for this query.' Only attempt client-side matching for single-term queries with simple ASCII characters. Document the limitation explicitly.

objections.description: Section 3 states 'body reading: 1rem (16px floor enforced)' and also 'Font size: 16 / 18 / 20px (3 stops)'. If 16px is the floor, the user control starting at 16px makes sense, but the specification doesn't clarify whether the user selection overrides the floor or if the floor applies to the minimum control value. Additionally, 'Mobile density: achieved through collapsible sections and tighter vertical padding — never by reducing font size below 14px for any interactive element' contradicts the 16px floor.
objections.suggested_fix: Clarify the type scale hierarchy: base reading size (user-controllable: 16/18/20px), minimum interactive element size (14px absolute minimum, but prefer 16px), and mobile density approach (padding reduction, not type reduction). Specify that user font size selection only applies to document body text, not UI chrome.

objections.description: 'Show 5 most recent publications from each section' and '234 new publications today' require API endpoints that may not exist. The backend audit doesn't include checking for 'recent publications' or 'daily counts' endpoints. The plan assumes these exist without verification.
objections.suggested_fix: Add 'recent publications endpoint' and 'publication counts by date' to the Section 0 backend audit checklist. If these don't exist, the empty state should degrade gracefully: show only the search input with a static prompt, no dynamic data. This is a hidden dependency that could block the entire empty state design.

objections.description: Snap points at 40% viewport height on a 375px mobile screen = 150px, which may not be enough for a usable TOC. The handle zone at 48px might be difficult for users with motor impairments. No specification for virtual keyboard handling — when a sheet contains an input field and the keyboard appears, the sheet should resize or scroll.
objections.suggested_fix: Change TOC sheet minimum snap to 50% on viewports under 400px. Increase handle zone to 56px. Add explicit behavior for sheets containing inputs: when keyboard appears, sheet should auto-snap to maximum height and scroll to the focused input. Specify `visualViewport` API usage for keyboard detection.

objections.description: 'Remove separate Home route' but the document viewer is a separate route. The navigation model is unclear: user is on Search (home), taps a result, goes to /document/:id — where is the back button? How does the bottom tab bar behave on the document route? If 'Search' tab is tapped while on document, does it save position and return home?
objections.suggested_fix: Specify navigation flow explicitly: Document viewer has its own header with back arrow that returns to search with scroll position preserved. Bottom tab bar remains visible on document route but Search tab acts as a 'home' button (prompts to save reading position, then navigates). Add a navigation diagram.

suggestions.description: The plan touches many components that share state (search query, active filters, reading positions, saved documents). No specification for state management approach: React Context, Zustand, Jotai, or URL as source of truth. This architectural decision should be explicit before implementation begins.

orchestrator_response.content:
# DOU Search & Reading App — UI/Product Execution Plan v3

---

## 0. Prerequisite: Backend Capability Audit

**Blocking gate. No feature work begins until this is complete.**

Use gabi-es to answer these specific questions about the current Elasticsearch backend:

**Document structure:**
- What fields exist on a DOU document? (title, body, organ, section, type, date, hierarchy structure)
- What does the document body look like — flat HTML, structured JSON with article/paragraph/inciso nesting, or raw text?
- What is the document size distribution? Measure P50, P90, P99 word counts and maximum nesting depth (articles, parágrafos, incisos, alíneas).
- Are cross-references pre-indexed as a field, or must they be extracted from body text?

**Search capabilities:**
- Does the API return faceted aggregation counts for organ, type, section, date range?
- Does search return highlight fragments with matched terms?
- What query relaxation or "did you mean" capabilities exist?
- What is the pagination model — cursor-based, offset-based, or unbounded result sets?
- Are there rate limits? What are typical response times for large-corpus queries (P50, P95)?

**Content endpoints:**
- Does a "recent publications" or "daily publication count" endpoint exist?
- Does the backend support streaming responses for large documents?

**Output:** A `docs/backend-capabilities.md` file with three columns: "Available today", "Requires backend work", and "Impact on frontend scope." Every subsequent step references this file, not assumptions.

**Critical dependency:** If §0 reveals flat text bodies with no field-level filtering, the viewer timebox increases by 3 days for parser development and the search timebox decreases by 2 days (fewer filter features). If no recent-publications endpoint exists, the empty state degrades to a static search prompt. These contingencies are reflected in the risk register (§12).

**Timebox:** 1 day.
**MCP:** gabi-es exclusively.
**Done when:** The capabilities doc exists, is reviewed, search/viewer UX tiers are assigned, and document size distribution data is recorded.

---

## 1. Foundation Layer

### Component Framework
Keep shadcn/ui as the structural layer. Use React Bits only for targeted motion (page transitions, sheet entrances). Do not adopt Tailark. Ground every decision in existing components: SearchPage, DocumentPage, BottomSheet, CommandPalette, DocumentGraph, MobileActionsBar.

### State Management
URL is the source of truth for search state (query, filters, pagination). Zustand for client-side ephemeral state (sheet open/closed, reading mode toggle, active section). localStorage for persistence (reading positions, saved searches, typography preferences). No React Context for anything that crosses more than two component levels — Zustand stores are cheaper and debuggable.

**Deliverable:** `src/stores/` directory with `searchStore.ts`, `readingStore.ts`, `preferencesStore.ts`. URL sync via a custom `useURLSearchParams` hook that bidirectionally syncs Zustand search state with `window.location.search`.

---

## 2. Auth Model & Persistence Strategy

**MVP is unauthenticated.** All user state lives in localStorage.

### Reading Position Schema (v1)
```
{
  _schemaVersion: 1,
  documentId: string,
  documentHash: string,          // publication date + ID, for staleness detection
  lastSectionId: string,         // primary resume mechanism — non-nullable for structured docs
  scrollOffsetWithinSection: number,  // px offset within the section for precise resume
  scrollPercentFallback: number, // fallback for documents where parsing failed
  timestamp: number
}
```

### Saved Searches Schema (v1)
```
{
  _schemaVersion: 1,
  query: string,
  filters: Record<string, string[]>,
  createdAt: number
}
```

### Persistence Rules
- Cap at 100 reading positions (LRU eviction) and 50 saved searches.
- All localStorage operations wrapped in try/catch. On `QuotaExceededError`: evict oldest 20% of reading positions, retry. If still fails, degrade to sessionStorage for current session with a non-modal toast: "Storage full. Reading position will not be saved."
- Schema migration utility runs on app init: reads `_schemaVersion`, applies migrations sequentially. Unknown versions are discarded with a console warning.
- Staleness detection: if `documentHash` doesn't match current document metadata, discard the reading position and don't show the resume prompt.

### Post-MVP Migration Path
When backend user service ships: a `migrateLocalStorageToBackend()` function runs on first authenticated session, uploads all reading positions and saved searches, then clears localStorage copies. Design the unauthenticated experience so it feels complete, not degraded.

---

## 3. Visual Direction — Concrete Specification

Engineering decisions that ship as `src/styles/design-tokens.ts` and corresponding CSS custom properties.

### Typography
- **UI typeface:** Inter (already likely bundled via shadcn). Weights: 400, 500, 600.
- **Reading typeface:** Source Serif 4 (variable, loaded on document route only via dynamic import). Subset to Latin Extended for Brazilian Portuguese. Use `font-display: swap`. Preload with `<link rel="preload">` at low priority on the search page to warm the cache before document navigation. Weights: 400, 600.
- **Type scale (rem):** 0.75 / 0.8125 / 0.875 / 1.0 / 1.125 / 1.25 / 1.5 / 2.0 — 8 stops, no freestyle sizes.
- **Document body text:** User-controllable at 16 / 18 / 20px (3 stops). 16px is the minimum. Line-height: 1.6 / 1.7 / 1.8 (user-controllable). Max-width: 38rem (~65 characters at 16px).
- **UI chrome text:** Minimum 14px for interactive elements (buttons, chips, tabs). Minimum 12px for non-interactive metadata (timestamps, badges). These are not user-controllable.
- **Mobile density:** Achieved through collapsible sections and tighter vertical padding (8px → 4px on compact elements) — never by reducing font size.

### Color — Dark-first with Light Mode Token Architecture
- **Dark theme neutral scale:** Warm gray anchored at `hsl(30, 4%, N%)` where N ranges 6% (background-deepest — raised from 4% for OLED distinguishability from true black) to 85% (foreground-primary). 10 stops.
- **Light theme neutral scale:** Defined in the same token file but shipped dark-only in MVP. Anchored at `hsl(30, 4%, N%)` where N ranges 98% (background-deepest) to 15% (foreground-primary). Semantic mapping reverses: what's `surface-1` in dark maps to the equivalent elevation in light. Defining both scales now prevents painful retrofitting later.
- **Accent:** Teal — `hsl(172, 50%, 42%)` primary, `hsl(172, 40%, 32%)` muted, `hsl(172, 55%, 52%)` bright. Used only for: interactive element focus, active filter chips, reading progress indicator, primary CTAs. No accent in body text or structural borders.
- **Semantic colors:** Error `hsl(0, 65%, 55%)`, warning `hsl(38, 80%, 55%)`, success `hsl(145, 50%, 45%)`.
- **WCAG AA enforcement:** Every token pair (foreground on background) annotated with computed contrast ratio as a comment in the token file. CI validation script (`scripts/validate-contrast.ts`) fails the build if any text pair < 4.5:1 or any interactive element border < 3:1. Mid-range combinations (e.g., muted text on elevated surface) must be explicitly validated — warm neutrals at low saturation are failure-prone.

### Deliverable
`src/styles/design-tokens.ts` exporting all values for both dark and light scales. `src/styles/tokens.css` with CSS custom properties. `scripts/validate-contrast.ts` runnable in CI.

**Timebox:** 2 days (tokens + contrast validation script + one representative component — search result card — as hierarchy validation).
**MCP:** ui-copilot for typeface pairing validation.
**Done when:** Token file exists, CSS vars are wired, one search result card renders with new tokens, contrast validation passes in CI, both dark and light scales are defined (only dark is applied).

---

## 4. Bottom Sheet Primitives

**Sequenced before search and viewer because both depend on sheets.**

### Implementation Strategy
Start with a custom implementation using the variant prop pattern. If gesture handling on iOS Safari proves fragile within 2 days, fall back to [vaul](https://github.com/emilkowalski/vaul) which handles overscroll edge cases. This is a named decision, not a hope.

### 4a. Selector Sheet (TOC, single-filter pickers)
- **Snap points:** 50% / 85% viewport height (raised from 40% — 150px on a 375px screen is unusable for TOC).
- **Behavior:** Selecting an item dismisses sheet to 0% and triggers navigation. No sticky footer.

### 4b. Editor Sheet (multi-filter editing, saved search config)
- **Snap points:** 60% / 95% viewport height.
- **Behavior:** Sticky footer with Apply / Reset buttons, always visible. Apply dismisses and triggers search. Reset clears sheet state but keeps sheet open.
- **Keyboard handling:** When a sheet contains an input and the virtual keyboard appears, auto-snap to maximum height and scroll to the focused input. Use `visualViewport` API for keyboard detection on mobile.

### 4c. Action Sheet (share, bookmark, typography controls)
- **Snap points:** Auto-height only (content-fit, max 50% viewport).
- **Behavior:** No internal scroll. Tap action → execute → dismiss. Tap outside → dismiss.

### All Sheets — Non-Negotiable Behaviors
- **Handle zone:** 56px (increased for motor accessibility). Drag on handle controls sheet position. Below handle, internal content scrolls normally.
- **Overscroll containment:** `overscroll-behavior: contain` on inner scroll containers. `touch-action: none` on handle zone. These are the two CSS properties that prevent the "sheet moves when inner scroll hits boundary" jank on iOS Safari.
- **Viewport units:** Use `dvh` (dynamic viewport height) with `vh` fallback for browsers that don't support it. This handles iOS Safari's collapsing address bar.
- **Focus trapping:** On open, focus moves to first interactive element. Tab cycles within sheet. Escape dismisses. Focus restored to trigger element on close.
- **Screen reader:** `role="dialog"`, `aria-modal="true"`, `aria-label` describing context.
- **Backdrop:** `rgba(0,0,0,0.5)`, tap-to-dismiss.
- **Animation:** `transition: transform 300ms cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1)`. Respects `prefers-reduced-motion` (instant snap, no spring).
- **Reduced motion:** All spring animations replaced with instant transitions.

**Files touched:** `src/components/BottomSheet.tsx` → refactor into single component with `variant` prop (`selector | editor | action`).

**Timebox:** 5 days (increased from 3 to account for iOS Safari/Chrome Android cross-browser debugging).
**MCP:** ui-copilot for gesture conflict patterns.
**Done when:** All three variants render correctly on 375px viewport. Tested manually on iOS Safari 16+ and Chrome Android 120+. Gesture conflicts resolved (inner scroll doesn't move sheet). Focus trapping works with keyboard only. Virtual keyboard doesn't break editor sheet.

---

## 5. Document Viewer — The Core Product

**This is where users spend 80% of their time. Sequenced as the first feature workstream. Implementation design for §5a is finalized AFTER §0 audit completes — the two paths below are contingency plans, not parallel work.**

### 5a. Legal Hierarchy Rendering

**Path A — Backend returns structured JSON:**
- Render as indented tree with left-border depth indicators (1px accent-muted per nesting level).
- Each article is a discrete block with an anchor ID: `section-art-5`, `section-art-5-p-3`.
- Collapse parágrafos/incisos under article headers on initial load for documents over 50 articles. Expand on tap.

**Path B — Backend returns flat HTML/text:**
- Build a dedicated parsing service (`src/services/douParser.ts`) that produces an AST, not inline regex replacements.
- **Before writing the parser:** Pull 20 diverse real documents via gabi-es (varying organs, decades, sections 1/2/3) and create `tests/fixtures/dou-structure/` directory with expected parse outputs.
- Parser must handle: `Art.` / `ART.` / `Artigo`, `§` / `Parágrafo único`, Roman numerals (I–L, upper and lowercase), letter alíneas (a–z), suffixed articles (`Art. 5º-A`), and editorial notes (retificações).
- **Acceptance threshold:** Parser correctly identifies article boundaries in ≥90% of the 20 test fixtures. Documents that fail parsing render as plain formatted text with a visible "Visualização estruturada indisponível" indicator — not silent degradation.
- Add 2 days to the viewer timebox for parser development and test fixture creation.

### 5b. Table of Contents

Generated from document structure (parsed AST or structured JSON), not from generic heading extraction.

- On mobile: Selector Sheet (variant 4a) triggered from MobileActionsBar.
- On desktop: Collapsible sidebar, 240px wide, sticky.
- Active section highlighted based on scroll position (IntersectionObserver on each article anchor).
- TOC items show: article number + first 60 characters of article text. Article number is never truncated.

### 5c. Search Term Highlighting

If the user arrived from search:

**Backend provides highlight data:** Highlight all matching terms using `<mark>` elements with accent-bright background at 20% opacity. "N matches found" indicator in sticky header with previous/next arrows. Highlighting dismissed explicitly via "Clear highlights" action.

**Backend does NOT provide highlight data:** Attempt client-side matching ONLY for single-term queries (no phrases, no boolean operators, no stemming). For multi-term or complex queries, show a toast: "Destaques indisponíveis para esta busca." This avoids misleading match counts and O(n) DOM traversal on large documents.

### 5d. Cross-Reference Linking

**MVP: Non-interactive styling only.** Parse reference patterns (`Art. N`, `Lei nº X/Y`, `Portaria nº Z`) with regex and render as visually distinct text (subtle underline, muted accent color) but non-interactive.

**Post-MVP (requires backend work):** Cross-reference resolution is precomputed at index time and stored as a field on the document. Add "Does the backend precompute cross-reference links?" to §0 audit. If available, render as tappable links. If the backend cannot precompute: extract all references client-side, fire a single `_msearch` batch query after initial render, then asynchronously upgrade styled text to links. Never block initial render on cross-reference resolution.

**Intra-document references** (e.g., "conforme o Art. 12 acima") are linked to section anchors within the same document. These are safe to resolve client-side since the section IDs are already in the DOM.

### 5e. URL Deep Linking

Route schema: `/document/:id#section-:sectionId`

- On load: if hash is present, scroll to that section after render.
- Share button generates URL with current section's hash.
- Browser back/forward navigates between section anchors within the document.

### 5f. Progressive Rendering & Performance

DOU documents can exceed 50,000 words. The rendering strategy is tiered based on the §0 document size distribution data.

**Tier 1 — Documents under 5,000 words (~P50):**
Full render. No chunking. Browser Ctrl+F works completely.

**Tier 2 — Documents 5,000–20,000 words (~P50–P90):**
Chunked append via IntersectionObserver. Render first 5 sections immediately; append remaining sections as user scrolls within 2 sections of them. Accept DOM node counts up to 8,000. Browser Ctrl+F works for rendered content; unrendered sections hydrate quickly enough on forward scroll that most Ctrl+F "find next" operations work. Log node counts to monitoring but do not alert.

**Tier 3 — Documents over 20,000 words (~P90+):**
Windowed rendering: render visible sections ±2, unmount sections more than 4 screens away. Browser Ctrl+F is explicitly degraded for these documents. Replace with an **in-app document search bar** (`src/components/DocumentSearch.tsx`) that searches the raw text/JSON model (kept in memory, never in DOM) and scrolls to matches, hydrating target sections on demand. The in-app search bar is shown automatically for Tier 3 documents and available via Cmd+F override (intercept the keyboard shortcut) or a search icon in the sticky header.

- Show a subtle banner on Tier 3 documents: "Documento extenso — use a busca interna (Ctrl+F) para localizar termos."
- **Memory management:** Unmounted sections release their DOM nodes. The raw text model (~1 byte per character) for a 50,000-word document is ~300KB — acceptable to keep in memory.
- **CSS containment:** All section wrappers use `contain: content` to isolate layout/paint costs.

**DocumentGraph (related documents):**
- Desktop: compact node map (max 50 nodes, paginated) in sidebar below TOC.
- Mobile: hidden by default, accessible via Action Sheet from MobileActionsBar. Simplified to a flat list of related document titles — no force-directed graph on 375px viewport.

### 5g. Reading Mode & Progress

- **Mobile:** Reading mode is the default — full-width content within 38rem max-width.
- **Desktop/Tablet:** Toggle via keyboard shortcut (Shift+Alt+R — avoids Alt+R browser conflicts) or header button. Hides sidebar, centers content, increases max-width to 42rem.
- **Keyboard shortcut reference:** All shortcuts (Cmd+K, Shift+Alt+R, Escape, arrow keys) are discoverable inside CommandPalette as a "Keyboard Shortcuts" tab.
- **Progress indicator:** 2px-tall fixed bar at top of viewport, accent-primary color, width proportional to scroll percentage. Also shown as percentage in sticky header. Uses `role="progressbar"` + `aria-valuenow`.
- **Reading resume:** On navigating away, save position to localStorage (schema from §2, using `lastSectionId` as primary mechanism). On returning: if `documentHash` matches, show a non-modal toast: "Continuar do Art. 15?" with Ir / Dispensar. Toast auto-dismisses after 5 seconds. If `documentHash` doesn't match (document was republished), discard stale position silently.

### 5h. Typography Controls

Accessible from MobileActionsBar (Action Sheet variant) or sticky header dropdown on desktop.

- Font size: 16 / 18 / 20px (3 stops). Applies only to document body text, not UI chrome.
- Line height: 1.6 / 1.7 / 1.8.
- Persisted to localStorage per user (not per document).
- **Discoverability:** On first document open (checked via localStorage flag), show a subtle dot badge on the typography action in MobileActionsBar. Badge clears on first tap.

### 5i. Copy with Citation

Text selection in the document body enhances the clipboard:
- On copy (intercept `copy` event): append citation metadata after the selected text: `\n— Art. 5, § 3º · DOU Seção 1, 15/03/2024`.
- Citation includes: article/section reference (if determinable from selection position), DOU section, publication date.
- If section reference can't be determined, fall back to document title + date.
- Plain text copy only (no HTML in clipboard) to avoid formatting issues in destination apps.

### 5j. Error States

- **Document fetch failure (network error, 500):** Full-page error state with retry button. Message: "Não foi possível carregar o documento. Verifique sua conexão." Retry uses exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, max 3 attempts).
- **Document not found (404):** Error state with "Documento não encontrado" and link back to search.
- **Section parse failure (Path B):** Inline error placeholder within document body: "Esta seção não pôde ser processada" in a muted callout box. Surrounding sections render normally.
- **Network loss during reading:** Continue showing already-rendered content. Show a non-dismissable banner at top: "Sem conexão — conteúdo já carregado permanece disponível." Hide banner when connection restores.

**Files touched:** `src/pages/DocumentPage.tsx`, new `src/components/DocumentRenderer.tsx`, `src/components/DocumentTOC.tsx`, `src/components/DocumentGraph.tsx` (refactor), `src/components/ReadingProgress.tsx`, `src/components/TypographyControls.tsx`, `src/components/SearchHighlight.tsx`, `src/components/DocumentSearch.tsx` (in-app search for Tier 3), `src/components/DocumentError.tsx`, `src/services/douParser.ts` (Path B only), `tests/fixtures/dou-structure/` (Path B only).

**Timebox:** 10 days (8 base + 2 for parser/test fixtures if Path B). Add 2 days for integration testing and bug fixes = **12 days total.**
**MCP:** gabi-es for document structure audit and cross-reference patterns. ui-copilot for reading layout. json-render for viewer layout prototyping.
**Done when:** A real DOU document (>10,000 words) renders on a 375px viewport with: TOC navigation working, search highlights visible (backend-provided), progress bar accurate, resume prompt functional, font controls operational, in-app search functional for Tier 3 documents. One VoiceOver test session completed for article navigation. Lighthouse performance score ≥ 80 on mobile throttling.

---

## 6. Search — The Acquisition Loop

### 6a. Tier 1: Available Backend Capabilities (ship first)

Based on the §0 audit, implement UX only for what gabi-es actually returns today.

**Pagination model (determined by §0 audit):**
- If server-side paginated (expected): render the page directly — no virtualization needed. Page/cursor encoded in URL params (`?q=...&page=2`). 20 results per page.
- If unbounded result sets (unlikely): use `@tanstack/react-virtual`, but add this as a contingency, not a default. Note: virtualized lists break browser Ctrl+F — acceptable for search results since the in-page search use case is less critical here than in document viewing.

**Result card redesign:**
- Show: document title (Source Serif 4, 600 weight), organ tag, publication date, section badge, 2-line snippet with highlighted terms.
- Density: ~4 results visible on 375px viewport without scrolling.
- Skeleton cards shown within 300ms of query submission (before results return). 4 skeleton cards matching result card dimensions.

**Filter UI:**
- Date range: inline date picker in sticky search header (collapsed to "Data: Todas" chip).
- Type/Section: chip bar below search input showing active filters. Tap chip → Selector Sheet.
- All active filters encoded in URL query parameters for shareability and browser back support.
- URL is the source of truth for filter state — Zustand syncs bidirectionally.

**Loading states:**

## 7. App Shell & Navigation

**Sequenced after viewer and search because tokens and sheets have been battle-tested on real components.**

### Navigation Model
- Home page IS the search page. One route: `/`. Entering a query adds `?q=...&page=1` to the URL without a page navigation.
- Bottom tab bar on mobile: 2 items — Busca (home) and Salvos (bookmarked documents + saved searches from localStorage).
- **Document viewer route** (`/document/:id`): bottom tab bar remains visible. Search tab acts as home button (saves reading position to localStorage first, then navigates). Back arrow in document header returns to search with scroll position preserved (search results page scroll position stored in sessionStorage).
- No analytics tab. No settings tab. Typography controls are contextual in the viewer; there is no global settings page.

### CommandPalette
- Keyboard shortcut: Cmd+K / Ctrl+K. Fully keyboard-navigable. Focus trapping.
- On mobile: triggered via search icon in header, opens as full-screen sheet.
- Suggestion sources: recent searches + recently read document titles.
- Includes a "Atalhos de teclado" tab listing all keyboard shortcuts.

**Files touched:** `src/App.tsx` (routing), `src/components/AppShell.tsx`, `src/pages/SearchPage.tsx` (absorbs Home), `src/components/MobileActionsBar.tsx` (2 tabs), `src/components/CommandPalette.tsx` (accessibility audit + shortcuts tab).

**Timebox:** 4 days.
**Done when:** App has one primary route serving as both home and search. Navigation is 2-tab on mobile. CommandPalette passes keyboard-only navigation test. Document viewer back-navigation preserves search scroll position.

**Font strategy:** Source Serif 4 subset (Latin Extended, ~40KB woff2) loaded via dynamic import on document route. `font-display: swap` to avoid blocking render. Low-priority `<link rel="preload">` injected on search page to warm cache. Font loading state: body text renders in Inter until Source Serif loads — no layout shift because both fonts are sized to the same metrics container.

## 10. Accessibility — WCAG 2.1 AA Floor

Not a nice-to-have. This is a government data product.

- **Contrast:** Automated CI validation via `scripts/validate-contrast.ts`. Every foreground/background token pair annotated with computed ratio. Build fails if any text pair < 4.5:1 or interactive border < 3:1.
- **Focus management:** Visible focus ring (2px accent-primary outline, 2px offset) on all interactive elements. Bottom sheets and CommandPalette trap focus on open, restore on close.
- **Screen readers:** Bottom sheets: `role="dialog"` + `aria-modal`. Search results: `role="list"` + `role="listitem"`. Document sections: `aria-label` with article numbers. Progress bar: `role="progressbar"` + `aria-valuenow`. Legal hierarchy: articles use `<article>` element, nested lists use `<ol>` with `aria-label` for context.
- **Keyboard navigation:** All features usable without mouse. Tab order follows visual order. Escape closes any overlay. Arrow keys navigate TOC items and search results.
- **Reduced motion:** `prefers-reduced-motion` disables all spring animations and page transitions. Instant snap for sheets.
- **Manual testing:** At least one VoiceOver (iOS) and one TalkBack (Android) test session for the document viewer. Document expected screen reader output for article navigation in `docs/accessibility-testing.md`.
